2002-11-01.title: Theft Charges Dropped Against Diana's Former Butler
2002-11-01.abstract: The court was informed that the butler had told Queen Elizabeth II he was keeping some of the princess's belongings.

2002-11-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: No More Late Nights For Parliament
2002-11-01.abstract: British Parliament votes to end centuries-old ritual of late-night sessions and shift workday timetable three hours earlier; sessions will now begin at 11:30 am and run no later than 7:30 pm (S)

2002-11-01.title: Qaeda Uses Teeming Karachi As New Base, Pakistanis Say
2002-11-01.abstract: Pakistani officials say Al Qaeda members are trying to re-establish their network in Karachi, with support from local people, elaborate secrecy and Internet communication; say story of Ramzi bin al-Shibh's final month of freedom and his chaotic arrest illustrates why it is proving so difficult to eliminate Al Qaeda; say bin al-Shibh, thought to be key planner of Sept 11 terrorist attacks on World Trade Center and Pentagon, lived quietly in Karachi, 10 miles from American Consulate, and spent his days with Qaeda friends, logging on to Internet and at night playing cards; Pakistani officials are convinced that Al Qaeda's operations head Khalid Shaikh Mohammed remains in Karachi, hiding in apartment, just as bin al-Shibh did; Mohammed, one of America's most sought men, is alleged mastermind of Sept 11 attacks; Pakistani officials say bin al-Shibh's arrest disrupted Al Qaeda's network in Karachi but did not eliminate it; yearlong crackdown by Gen Pervez Musharraf has failed to stop Pakistani militants from aiding Qaeda members; photo (L)

2002-11-01.title: World Briefing | Asia: North Korea: Remains Of Americans Returned
2002-11-01.abstract: United States troops near Pyongyang, North Korea, accept remains of 11 american servicemen killed in Korean War (S)

2002-11-01.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-11-01.abstract: BRITAIN: No More Late Nights For Parliament; AUSTRIA: Leader Of Rightist Party Resigns;

2002-11-02.title: EXHIBITION:PARIS : Shedding some light on an era of invention
2002-11-02.abstract: The feverish outburst of artistic invention on the Paris scene from 1890 to 1900 is perhaps the most astonishing phenomenon in the recent history of Western culture. Some may marvel that it should have taken so long for an exhibition such as "Les Peintres Graveurs: 1890-1900" to see the light of day.

2002-11-02.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-11-02.abstract: SRI LANKA: Rebels Protest Leader'S Sentence; SOUTH KOREA: President'S Son Sentenced;.

2002-11-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Macedonia: Government Includes Ex-Rebels
2002-11-02.abstract: Macedonian Parliament votes in new governing coalition that includes party formed by ethnic Albanian guerrillas; nationalists accuse new prime minister Branko Crvenkovski of bringing 'terrorists' into government by giving ministries to party led by former rebel commander Ali Ahmeti; Crvenkovski says inclusion of party will help stabilize nation; photo (S)

2002-11-02.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-11-02.abstract: THE HAGUE: Milosevic Ailing Again; MACEDONIA: Government Includes Ex-Rebels; CZECH REPUBLIC: Post-Havel Plans; BELARUS: New Law Restricts Religions; BULGARIA: Missile Destruction For Nato;.

2002-11-02.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Judges Shut Another Research Group
2002-11-02.abstract: Iran's hard-line judiciary closes second research group, Ayandeh Research Group, and arrests two researchers, one of whom is later freed; Hossein Ghazian is still being held (S)

2002-11-03.title: Afghans Raise Concern That Taliban Forces Are Reorganizing in Pakistan
2002-11-03.abstract: Senior Afghan officials are increasingly concerned that Taliban forces are reorganizing in border areas of Pakistan in preparation for new wave of terrorist attacks on Afghanistan; concerns are heightened by elections in Pakistan in which fundamentalist Islamic parties allied with Taliban gained control of provincial governments along border; assessment is shared by many people in Pentagon and State Department, who are convinced that elements of Pakistani intelligence continue to funnel weapons and money to Taliban units and their allies inside and outside Afghanistan; photo (M)

2002-11-03.title: Law and Politics Likely to Curb Power of Brazil's New Leader
2002-11-03.abstract: Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva is likely to find his freedom of action circumscribed when he becomes president of Brazil on Jan 1 despite his landslide election victory; his powers are being trimmed by new legislation, and his lack of majority in Congress means initiatives he is proposing may end up being blocked or watered down (M)

2002-11-03.title: Egypt Shores Up Security, but Tourism Is Shaky
2002-11-03.abstract: Egypt's tourism industry, which accounts for 12 percent of economy and 1 in 7 jobs, is suffering because of Sept 11 attacks, prospect of war in Iraq and violence in Israel and West Bank, as well as economic troubles around the world; visits by Americans have plunged, and fewer visitors are going to historic sites like Luxor; security has been greatly increased since 1997, when Islamic militants massacred 58 tourists and 4 Egyptians at temple in Luxor and virtually shut down tourism; photos; map (M)

2002-11-03.title: Feud Grows Between Russia and Denmark Over Extradition of Chechen Rebel
2002-11-03.abstract: Denmark refuses to extradite Akhmed Zakayev, senior Chechen leader, saying Russian prosecutors have yet to provide evidence that he was involved in terrorist activities; some members of Russia's Parliament call for boycott of Danish goods, while others lead demonstration outside Danish Embassy in Moscow; relations between Denmark and Russia have soured badly since Danish authorities refused to shut down conference of Chechen representatives in Copenhagen after siege of Moscow theater by Chechen guerrillas (M)

2002-11-03.title: Climate Talks Shift Focus to How to Deal With Changes
2002-11-03.abstract: Round of international climate talks is held in New Delhi, and focus is on adapting to global warming instead of fighting to limit emissions of gases responsible for it; many environmentalists have long avoided discussing adaptation for fear it would smack of defeatism; shift is partly driven by unusual weather this year and fear that costs of weather-related disasters will rise with global warming; another impetus is rising realization that many significant shifts have already been set in motion by century-long accumulation of warming gases; photos (M)

2002-11-04.title: NETANYAHU AGREES TO A CABINET POST IF A VOTE FOLLOWS
2002-11-04.abstract: Former Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu conditionally agrees to become Israel's foreign minister; says he will accept offer by Prime Min Ariel Sharon if government promises to call new elections soon and if he reaches understanding on three major issues; cites exile for Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, which he favors, establishment of Palestinian state, which he opposes, and location of new fence along boundary of West Bank; he would replace Shimon Peres, architect of Oslo accords and supporter of two-state solution to conflict with Palestinians; offer by Sharon is latest move to recruit allies and box in rivals to shore up his minority coalition government now that Labor Party has dropped out; collapse of 19-month-old unity government in budget dispute and political implications of overture to rival Netanyahu discussed; photo (M),

2002-11-04.title: VOTERS IN TURKEY EXPEL LEADERSHIP
2002-11-04.abstract: Turkish voters elect former Islamicist Recep Tayyip Erdogan to head new government, expelling ruling coalition led by Prime Min Bulent Ecevit; his Justice and Development Party wins 34.2 percent of vote, compared with 19.4 for closest rival; he quickly seeks to calm fears by emphasizing his support for moderate, secular Turkey, committed to joining European Union and continuing strong relations with US; map (M)

2002-11-04.title: White House Rejects North Korean Offer for Talks
2002-11-04.abstract: White House rejects North Korean offer to open talks on North's newly disclosed nuclear weapons program, demanding program be dismantled first (S)

2002-11-04.title: Colombia Takes Aim At Rebels In Its Cities
2002-11-04.abstract: Colombia's new law-and-order Pres Alvaro Uribe moves to pacify Communa 13, district of Medellin that is epitome of urban violence; photo; map; success could become model for other big citieshard hit by 38-year-old civil conflict (M)

2002-11-04.title: India Reports Killing 2, Stopping Terror Attack
2002-11-04.abstract: Police in New Delhi report thwarting terrorist attack on crowded shopping center on eve of Hindu festival; photo; accused are said to be Pakistan-based Islamic militants (S)

2002-11-05.title: North Korea Is Ready to Get Fuel Shipment Under 1994 Pact That It Violated
2002-11-05.abstract: Multinational organization that administers 1994 agreement between United States and North Korea prepares to send shipment of fuel oil to North Korea, despite North's recent admission that it violated pact by operating secret nuclear weapons program; new shipment by agency, Korean Peninsula Energy Development Organization, underscores difficulties Bush administration is facing in its efforts to isolate North Korea over its weapons program (M)

2002-11-05.title: U.S. Refines Resolution on Iraq as Hopes Rise
2002-11-05.abstract: Bush administration officials work to put finishing touches on Security Council resolution for tough weapons inspections in Iraq, and major Council nations say they are optimistic that measure will be adopted soon and with broad support (M)

2002-11-05.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Officials Quit After Suspect's Death
2002-11-05.abstract: South Korean Justice Min Kim Jung Kil and Prosecutor General Lee Myung Jae resign and apologize for murder suspect's death during Seoul police interrogation; relatives of Cho Cheun Hun hold he was tortured to death; civic groups call for full inquiry; photo (S)

2002-11-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Czech Republic: Government Loses Senate Edge
2002-11-05.abstract: Czech Republic's governing coalition loses majority in Senate, diminishing chance they will decide who succeeds outgoing Pres Vaclav Havel (S)

2002-11-05.title: Moscow Journal; Come Home, Little Squirrel: Parks Have Vacancies
2002-11-05.abstract: European red squirrel, once common in Moscow's parks, have dwindled down to about 200, and city introduces breeding program to bring them back; photos (M)

2002-11-06.title: U.S. Would Use Drones To Attack Iraqi Targets
2002-11-06.abstract: American military officials say that in war with Iraq, Air Force would use same missile-firing drones that killed one of Al Qaeda's senior leaders in Yemen to attack Iraqi air defense radars, mobile Scud missile launchers and possibly sensitive targets in Baghdad (M)

2002-11-06.title: Royal Secrets Come to Light As Butler's Tale Spills Out
2002-11-06.abstract: Collapse of court case against Paul Burrell, royal butler accused of stealing hundreds of items belonging to Britain's Princess Diana, has not forestalled disclosure of embarrassing royal secrets; Burrell himself has been fielding offers from British news media, and The Sun has published torrent of revelations said to be based on his 39-page deposition to police (S)

2002-11-06.title: Iran Pardons Cleric Who Was Jailed for Insulting Islam
2002-11-06.abstract: Iran's supreme religious leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei pardons Abdullah Nouri, veteran cleric who three years ago had been jailed on charges of insulting Islam; Nouri was considered leading reformer at time of his arrest (S)

2002-11-06.title: World Briefing | Asia: Vietnam: Firefighting Plans After Deadly Blaze
2002-11-06.abstract: Ho Chi Minh City announces financial investment of over $9 million dollars in equipment and training for firefighters; new initiative follows fire in Vietnamese city department store that killed at least 60 people (S)

2002-11-06.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: 'Soccer Candidate' Creates Party
2002-11-06.abstract: Chung Mong Joon, popular president of national soccer association, creates political party and is named candidate for South Korea's December presidential elections (S)

2002-11-07.title: Report of Arms Sale by Ukraine to Iraq Causes Consternation
2002-11-07.abstract: Report by American and British investigators reinforces suspicions that Ukraine secretly sold advanced air-defense equipment to Iraq more than two years ago; some Ukrainian legislators express consternation; Pres Leonid D Kuchma denies supplying Iraq with 'any weapons at all' and calls for special commission of Security Council to undertake its own inquiry into allegations; Kuchma is heard to approve proposal to sell advanced radar system to Iraq in recordings of his private conversations that were secretly taped and smuggled to US more than year ago by presidential security guard (M)

2002-11-07.title: Graft in Eastern Europe Is Called Rampant
2002-11-07.abstract: Report by Open Society Institute, nonprofit organization, states that corruption is rampant in Central and Eastern European countries expecting to join wealthy European Union, and is likely to persist after union expands eastward in 2004 unless EU significantly strengthens its own anticorruption measures; report, based on yearlong study, finds that three-fourths of citizens in candidate states believe that most or all of their public officials are corrupt; says corruption in legislative process is probably biggest problem facing post-Communist countries, along with kickbacks and payoffs in public procurement contracts; one bright spot is Lithuania, which adopted national anticorruption strategy in Jan and which report says could serve as model for other countries; Transparency International, anti-corruption organization, ranks current EU members Italy and Greece as more corrupt than EU applicants Slovenia and Estonia in its current corruption perception index (M)

2002-11-07.title: Indonesian Police Say Suspect Admits Role in Bali Bombing
2002-11-07.abstract: The Indonesian police said today that they had arrested a man who admitted to planting the car bomb that blew up a Bali nightclub in a terrorist attack last month.

2002-11-07.title: North Sea Cod Crisis Brings Call for Nations to Act
2002-11-07.abstract: Number of North Sea cod is believed to be at its lowest level ever, half of what was considered minimum for assured propagation; scientists are warning that unless almost all commmercial fishing in North Sea is banned, cod stocks might soon collapse; cod crisis is one of many facing international community as countries compete for thinning stocks in world's once fish-thick seas; fish are last food source hunted on large scale in wild; United Nations warns that world's 17 main fisheres are being fished at or above sustainable levels; cod, once known as 'beef of the sea,' is world's most popular food fish; photo (M)

2002-11-07.title: Cairo Journal; Saddam's 'Weapons' Costly, 'Bush-Sharon' Cheap
2002-11-07.abstract: Thrifty Egyptians purchase their holiday treats at wholesale market in Cairo on first day of Ramadan; it is tradition for purveyors to give pet names to their dates, nuts and other delicacies for sale; 'Yasir Arafat' and 'Osama bin Laden' dates are biggest sellers; 'Mass Destruction' moniker given to date imported from Iraq is considered too costly by most shoppers; photo (M)

2002-11-08.title: Text of President Bush's Remarks on Security Council Vote
2002-11-08.abstract: The following is the text of President Bush's remarks on Friday following the U.N. Security Council vote to force the disarmament of Iraq, as distributed by The Associated Press.

2002-11-08.title: U.S., Pushing for Broader Ban, Blocks U.N. Anti-Cloning Move
2002-11-08.abstract: US, backed by 36 other countries, blocks German-French proposal at UN for worldwide ban on cloning to create human beings; insists that ban include all forms of human cloning(S)

2002-11-08.title: Iran Sentences Reformist to Death For Insult to Prophet Muhammad
2002-11-08.abstract: Iran's hard-line judiciary sentences outspoken reform activist Hashem Aghajari to death, 8 years in jail, 74 lashes and ten-year ban from teaching for allegedly insulting Prophet Mohammad by saying Muslims should not follow religious leaders 'blindly'; photo (S)

2002-11-08.title: Dutch Say Arrest Foiled Suicide Strike
2002-11-08.abstract: Dutch authorities in Eindhoven report arrest of 22-year-old Moroccan they believe was planning suicide attack (S)

2002-11-08.title: Syria Fears Isolation More Than War
2002-11-08.abstract: Syria, which has long centered foreign policy around its leadership of countries against Israel, fears being isolated in region if pro-US government is installed in Iraq; also fears being next on US list; photo (M)

2002-11-09.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-11-09.abstract: THE HAGUE: Effort To Speed Milosevic Trial; SWITZERLAND: Inquiry On Ex-Argentine Leader; NORTHERN IRELAND: Man Released In Spy Case; RUSSIA: More Ill From Tainted Dairy Products;.

2002-11-09.title: Transcript of Bush's Remarks on the Security Council's Iraq Resolution
2002-11-09.abstract: WASHINGTON, Nov. 8 — Following is the White House text of President Bush's remarks today after the Security Council approved the resolution on Iraq:.

2002-11-09.title: Chinese Pilgrims Flock to Jiang Family Home
2002-11-09.abstract: Childhood home of Chinese leader Jiang Zemin, Yangzhou, has become shrine of sorts, with visitors arriving daily to view his prosperous origins; Jiang, who transformed Chinese Communism by embracing entrepreneurs and intellectuals, came from cultured middle-class family of doctors, scholars and businessmen; seems ambivalent about new attention from market-driven media and unofficial biographers after being happily lionized for years with propaganda that omitted personal details; map; photos (M)

2002-11-09.title: World Briefing | Americas: Chile: Court Preserves Pinochet's Immunity
2002-11-09.abstract: Chile's Supreme Court rejects bid by Argentine judge to strip former dictator Augusto Pinochet of his official immunity so he can be questioned about 1974 assassination in Buenos Aires of Gen Carlos Prats, his predecessor as Chilean Army commander; finds Pinochet, 86, is mentally incapable of taking part in probe (S)

2002-11-09.title: Ghana's Envoy to Moscow Hurt in Latest Racial Attack
2002-11-09.abstract: Maj Gen Francis Y Mahama, 60, Ghana's ambassador to Russia, says he and driver were attacked by group of young men while walking in Moscow park, latest victims of wave of racist attacks; says festive crowd did nothing to halt attack; attacks on foreigners, especially Africans and Asians, have become increasingly common in recent years in much of Russia, despite official appeals for tolerance; African envoys had earlier appealed to Foreign Ministry to do more to protect embassy employees and other Africans (M)

2002-11-10.title: Afghan Professor Tames a Wild State
2002-11-10.abstract: Hakim Taniwal, former sociology professor, has rousted Afghan warlord in Khost Province with army he raised and trained using local money; his original intent was to use persuasion to oust warlord, Padsha Khan Zadran, but after months of failed negotiations Taniwal concluded that words would not work; he borrowed $125,000 from local businessmen and raised army; he now speaks proudly of how his soldiers trounced Zadran's forces in brief battle in early Sept, sending them scurrying to hills; his triumph shows how much one can get for dollar in Afghanistan these days; former professor's success is bright spot for new national government that he supports as it struggles to pay salaries and begs for foreign aid, particularly from United States; photo (M)

2002-11-10.title: Israelis Kill Palestinian Suspected in Attacks, Then Lose a Soldier
2002-11-10.abstract: Israeli soldiers in Jenin shoot and kill Islamic Jihad leader Iyad Sawalha, accused of masterminding attacks on Israeli buses that killed 31 people; one soldier is killed and second wounded in roadside bombing in Gaza (S)

2002-11-10.title: The Hand That Feeds Milosevic's Defense
2002-11-10.abstract: Zdenko Tomanovic discusses role as 'virtual lawyer' for Slobodan Milosevic at war crimes trial in The Hague; interview; photo; former Serb leader is conducting own defense, but Tomanovic is legal associate who gathers piles of evidence, more than 150,000 documents and exhibits (M)

2002-11-10.title: In Bitter Race In Argentina, Peronists Trip Each Other
2002-11-10.abstract: Argentine politics remains as chaotic as ever, even as battered economy shows signs of finally hitting bottom; fierce dispute between Pres Eduardo Duhalde and former Pres Carlos Menem plunges governing Peronists into crisis threatening election timetable; Peronists cannot even agree when to hold presidential nominating primary; Duhalde's candidate, Carlos Reutemann, has declined to run, so Duhalde wants more time to stitch together coalition; beneficiary is Adolfo Rodriguez Saa, who is running as old-fashioned populist (M)

2002-11-10.title: WAR PLAN FOR IRAQ CALLS FOR BIG FORCE AND QUICK STRIKES
2002-11-10.abstract: Pres Bush reportedly sets Iraq war plan to begin with air campaign shorter than one in Persian Gulf war and feature quick ground actions to seize footholds and strikes to cut off leadership in Baghdad; plan would mass 200,000-250,000 troops for attack by air, land and sea, commencing with 'rolling start' of substantially fewer troops; calls for quick capture of territory that could be used as bases to funnel troops deeper into country, to ease pressure on nearby staging areas like Saudi Arabia; photo; Bush agrees with Gen Tommy Franks on large troop levels; air campaign would try to cut off Baghdad leadership in hopes of causing government collapse, while trying not to destroy civilian infrastructure; ground operations would likely be woven into opening stages of air war; Bush warns Iraqi generals of war crimes charges if they purposely sacrifice civilians; analysts warn Hussein prepares volunteer martyrs' brigades for suicide bombings and drawing sympathy in region, to show 'real civilian blood on Al Jazeera' (M)

2002-11-11.title: IRAQ INSPECTIONS RECEIVE APPROVAL FROM ARAB LEAGUE
2002-11-11.abstract: Members of Arab League meeting in Cairo, Egypt, voice collective support for new weapons inspections inside Iraq; want Arab experts on inspection teams; warn UN resolution should not be considered free pass for American military action; restate longstanding Arab position that Iraq must work with UN weapons inspectors; analysts say action suggests most governments in region back seeing Pres Saddam Hussein defanged, yet fear repercussions of another war in region; photos (M)

2002-11-11.title: As AIDS Spreads, India Is Still Struggling for a Workable Strategy
2002-11-11.abstract: Women with HIV fill two wards of former tuberculosis sanitarium in India's southern state of Tamil Nadu; most are wives, or widows, infected yaers ago by their husbands; many have watched their spouses sicken, and die; now their turn has come; number of new patients is so great that hospital has no more beds and offers patients straw mats on floor; Tamil Nadu has been at vanguard of AIDS epidemic in India, country with world's second-highest number of HIV cases; recognizing that India's epidemic is at pivotal point, Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation will announce $100 million commitment over 5 to 10 years to combat spread of HIV and AIDS in India; foundation hopes to focus especially on prevention among mobile populations--sex workers, truckers, migrants---who carry virus from state to state; photos (L)

2002-11-11.title: A Palestinian Attack Kills 5 on a Northern Kibbutz
2002-11-11.abstract: At least one Palestinian gunman attacks kibbutz in northern Israel near border with West Bank, killing five people hours after police report foiling suicide bombing in same area; Al Aksa Martyrs Brigades, militant offshoot of Yasir Arafat's Fatah movement, claims responsibility for attack; it comes even as representatives of Fatah and Hamas meet in Cairo to discuss proposed halt to attacks in Israel; photo; map (M)

2002-11-11.title: Gates Foundation to Give $100 Million to Fight AIDS in India
2002-11-11.abstract: Bill Gates announced today that the foundation he and his wife established will give more than $100 million to combat AIDS in India.

2002-11-11.title: Tempers Flare After U.S. Sends a Canadian Citizen Back to Syria on Terror Suspicions
2002-11-11.abstract: Maher Arar, Syrian-born Canadian citizen, has been deported back to Syria by United States authorities on suspicions of belonging to terrorist group; decision is testing American-Canadian relations; American officials claim Arar is member of Al Qaeda, but Canadians say they have no such information; Syrians are questioning him closely, although officially Syrian government has expressed outrage that he was deported to Syria instead of Canada (M)

2002-11-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Czech Republic: Prague Spring Treason Charge
2002-11-12.abstract: A former Communist Party leader was charged with treason for his suspected role in cutting off access to radio and television as Soviet troops invaded during the so-called Prague Spring in 1968. Karel Hoffman, 78, who later became head of the Czech Communists, was head of the telecommunications office at the time. He is accused of shutting down radio and television broadcasts before the invasion and of refusing to broadcast a condemnation of it from the reformist government.   Ian Fisher (NYT)

2002-11-12.title: World Briefing: Africa
2002-11-12.abstract: ETHIOPIA: Premier Sees 'Ghastly' Famine'; SOUTH AFRICA: More Whites Sought In Bombings;.

2002-11-12.title: Italy (Gasp!) Starts to Clear The Clouds Of Smokers
2002-11-12.abstract: Italian government considers what was once unthinkable: clamping down on tobacco users; Health Min Girolamo Sirchia pledges to purify indoor air; Italian Senate has approved antismoking measure forbidding cigar and cigarette smoking in public places; Pres Carlo Azeglio Ciampi has lent his support to measure (M)

2002-11-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Sweden: Russians Expelled In Spy Case
2002-11-12.abstract: The government expelled two Russian diplomats for espionage in a widening diplomatic dispute said to involve spying at a subsidiary of the Ericsson telecommunications company. Press reports in Sweden have said the diplomats, who were not identified, were involved in industrial espionage related to Ericsson's military contracts. Russia's foreign ministry said it would respond appropriately, signaling tit-for-tat expulsions of Swedish diplomats in Moscow.   Steven Lee Myers (NYT)

2002-11-12.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-11-12.abstract: THE HAGUE: Milosevic Returns To Court; THE NETHERLANDS: Bosnia Inquiry Opens; GERMANY: Opposition Lashes Out At SchrÖDer; SWEDEN: Russians Expelled In Spy Case; CYPRUS: U.N. Peace Plan; POLAND: Low Turnout Prompts Concerns; CZECH REPUBLIC: Prague Spring Treason Charge;.

2002-11-13.title: Netanyahu Vows to Oust Arafat if Elected
2002-11-13.abstract: Benjamin Netanyahu vows to expel Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat if he is elected Israel's prime minister, speech to Likud Party convention, Tel Aviv; Prime Min Ariel Sharon casts himself as responsible centrist, winning loudest applause at convention; warns move against Arafat could strain United States ties; Israeli soldiers blow up home of Palestinian leader Muhammad Nayfeh, Shuwaika, West Bank; army says he sent gunman who killed five people at Kibbutz Metzer; Israeli forces backed by dozens of tanks and helicopters reportedly launch predawn raid on Nablus; photos (M)

2002-11-13.title: Commission to Investigate Moscow Siege Is Rejected
2002-11-13.abstract: Russia's lower house of parliament rejected two proposals today to create an independent commission to investigate the circumstances surrounding the 57-hour siege of a Moscow theater.

2002-11-13.title: World Briefing | Europe: The Hague: Milosevic Ailing Again
2002-11-13.abstract: Concern mounted about the fate of the trial of Slobodan Milosevic after it was halted again yesterday because of what court officials said was his exhaustion and high blood pressure. The judges at the United Nations war crimes tribunal have called on lawyers to find ways of cutting back the trial schedule and workload even further than they have done. Various proposals are being debated, including imposing a defense counsel on the former Yugoslav president or even releasing him temporarily. The court is expected to hold a hearing to discuss a solution, but for now, proceedings involving Mr. Milosevic are suspended. They had resumed on Monday for the first time since they were stopped on Nov. 1 because of his health problems.   Marlise Simons (NYT)

2002-11-13.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: Another Setback With North Korea
2002-11-13.abstract: The government said that human remains returned by North Korea that were supposed to be those of a kidnapped Japanese were of the wrong sex and age. The disagreement was the latest setback to efforts by the countries to normalize diplomatic relations after North Korea admitted kidnapping 13 Japanese to work in its spy programs beginning in the late 1970's. Japan said the remains, officially presented as those of a Japanese man abducted in 1980, were probably those of a woman 20 years older. North Korea has said 8 of the 13 abducted people are dead.   Howard W. French (NYT)

2002-11-13.title: Iraq Accepts United Nations Resolution; Weapons Inspectors Leave for Baghdad on Monday
2002-11-13.abstract: Iraq said today that it would "deal with" a resolution obliging it to disarm and allow United Nations weapons inspectors to begin work, but it denied that it possessed any prohibited weapons.

2002-11-14.title: A Guru, Visiting India, Is Welcomed to Seattle
2002-11-14.abstract: Young elite of India's high-tech revolution gather in Bangalore to greet Bill Gates on his first visit to India's technology capital, where his example has provided inspiration and where Microsoft is key customer, supplier and partner; photo; map; city, like Seattle before it, has become hub of enormous software services industry and home to thousands of young computer professions (M)

2002-11-14.title: Experts Say a bin Laden Impostor Could Fool a Lot of People
2002-11-14.abstract: Experts in voice authenticity says it is not surprising that officials cannot be absolutely certain that audiotape broadcast said to be by Osama bin laden is actually his voice; explain how imposter might imitate voice, which intelligence officials say is 90 percent likely to be bin Laden (M)

2002-11-14.title: IRAQ TELLS THE U.N. ARMS INSPECTIONS WILL BE PERMITTED
2002-11-14.abstract: Iraq says it will allow United Nations weapons inspectors to begin work; grudgingly agrees to 'deal with' Security Council resolution obligating it to disarm; nine-page letter, submitted from Iraqi Foreign Min Naji Sabri to Secretary General Kofi Annan, seethes with hostility toward United States and repeatedly denies Pres Bush's assertion that Iraq has weapons of mass destruction; Bush has no specific reaction to letter, saying only there will be 'no negotiations with Saddam Hussein'; further confrontation between Washington and Baghdad is expected; administration officials say they are content for now to wait until Dec 8--deadline for Iraq to submit complete list of its weapons programs--before making issue of violations; Annan urges patience from White House; some Security Council members view war as justified only if Iraq flagrantly violates new inspections regime; gap appears to be growing between those nations and Bush administration; letter from Iraq rages against Americans and British, calling them 'gang of evil'; photo (M)

2002-11-14.title: Family to Get Brain of German Radical
2002-11-14.abstract: German prosecutors say brain of urban guerrilla Ulrike Meinhof, which was removed after her suicide in 1976, will be returned to her family; daughter had filed lawsuit (S)

2002-11-14.title: China's President Steps Down to Make Way for New Generation
2002-11-14.abstract: Jiang Zemin's retirement as China's Communist party chief is confirmed as he triumphantly closes party congress glorifying his 13-year tenure (S)

2002-11-15.title: Mystery Man At the Helm Hu Jintao
2002-11-15.abstract: Man in the News profile of Hu Jintao, new leader of China; he is veteran Communist Party insider who never strays from party's prescribed oratory; his past suggests that he values power over vision; photo (M)

2002-11-15.title: French Police Oust Migrants Who Took Refuge in Church
2002-11-15.abstract: Tensions over immigration in France burst into open as police raid Catholic church in Calais, taking away in buses nearly 100 Iraqi Kurd and Afghan migrants who had taken refuge there; nine days ago, bowing to pressure from Britain, French government decreed that overcrowded refugee camp set up by Red Cross three years ago in nearby Sangatte would no longer accept new arrivals; goal is to stop thousands of migrants from risking their lives trying to get into Britain by jumping on high-speed trains through Channel Tunnel or as stowaways on ferries; map; photo (M)

2002-11-15.title: Italian Premier Names Rising Apprentice as Foreign Minister
Where the Violence Is Deadly and Daily, a Palestinian Camp Mourns Its Slain Children
2002-11-15.abstract: Posters of children killed by Israeli Army gunfire cover walls of alleys among bullet-scarred buildings of Palestinian refugee camp in Rafah, Gaza Strip, reminder that two 2-year-olds were shot dead there in three days this week; 8-year-old boy dies of wounds received last month in daily violence in Rafah (M)

2002-11-15.title: Little Headway In Terror War, Democrats Say
2002-11-15.abstract: Sen Bob Graham, Democrat and chairman of Senate Intelligence Committee, charges that Bush administration has been distracted from fight against terrorism by preparation for possible invasion of Iraq; American intelligence agencies come under renewed attack in Congress for failing to find Osama bin Laden; increasing certainty that he is still alive prompts senior Democratic senators to say effort to dismantle Al Qaeda is failure; Graham says American intelligence agencies must determine extent of terrorist threat at home before any military action is taken in Iraq; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld, in radio interview, underscores concerns that US could face increased dangers of terrorist attacks if Pres Bush orders military action against Iraq; says Pres Saddam Hussein might orchestrate such attacks; administration officials take FBI to task for warning issued on Nov 13 about possible attacks on hospitals, but FBI issues another warning, saying Al Qaeda's highest-priority targets remain within aviation, petroleum and nuclear sectors, as well as significant national landmarks (M)

2002-11-16.title: Pub Beer Is Still Warm, Even 24/7
2002-11-16.abstract: British government introduces legislation to allow pubs and bars to stay open 24 hours a day; hopes to put end to binge drinking that occurs when last call for drinks is made at 11 pm (S)

2002-11-16.title: 12 ISRAELIS KILLED IN HEBRON AMBUSH NEAR PRAYER SITE
2002-11-16.abstract: Twelve Israelis are killed in Hebron on West Bank when Palestinian snipers ambush Jewish settlers walking home from Sabbath prayers and then attack policemen, security guards and soldiers rushing to rescue; gunfight rages for more than three hours as Israeli rescue workers struggle to evacuate wounded; soldiers kill at least three Palestinians whom they identify as killers; Islamic Jihad claims responsibility for attack; Israeli leaders hold Palestinian leadership of Yasir Arafat responsible; photos; map (M)

2002-11-16.title: Broad Manhunt in Jordan Isolates, and Angers, a Town
2002-11-16.abstract: Maan, tribal town in desert in south Jordan, has been sealed off for nearly a week as police and army officials chase down Muhammad Ahmad al-Chalabi, who is known as Abu Sayyaf, small-time Islamic militant accused of heading defiant gang of drug and arms smugglers; operation is of scope rarely seen in Jordan, and people of Maan are angry; photos; map (M)

2002-11-16.title: ARTS & ANTIQUES: The lure of Expressionism
2002-11-16.abstract: LONDON—  As they took their seats in the room where Henry Wyndham, chairman of Sotheby's Europe, was about to dispose of the Beck collection of German Expressionist art on Oct. 8, few professionals felt sure of what would happen. This was the first serious sale of 20th-century paintings in the troubled economic environment of the opening season, followed a day later by another contingent of pictures from the same school. Would it, could it, work?

2002-11-16.title: Terror Alert Brings New Steps To Prevent Attacks, U.S. Says
2002-11-16.abstract: Condoleezza Rice, Pres Bush's national security adviser, says administration is taking extra precautions to protect nation's most critical facilities from terrorist assaults after FBI warning that Al Qaeda may be planning 'spectacular attacks' with mass casualties in US; rejects criticism from Senate Democrats that Bush's focus on preparations for war against Iraq is distracting him from campaign against terrorism; intelligence officials say senior Qaeda operative has been captured and taken into American custody (M)

2002-11-17.title: Schröder's Tax Surprise Angers Many Germans
2002-11-17.abstract: Germans are angry about raft of taxes on individuals and companies that Chancellor Gerhard Schroder has proposed in desperate effort to reduce budget deficit that has breached limit set by European Union; Schroder, who won re-election by reed-thin margin in September after campaign in which he said little about taxes, says dire condition of public finance became clear only after elections; photo; map (M)

2002-11-17.title: South Africa Urges the West To Ease Censure of Zimbabwe
2002-11-17.abstract: South Africa's foreign minister, Dr Nkosazana Zuma, meets with Zimbabwean counterpart, Stan Mudenge, and afterwards says Western nations should consider ending penalties they imposed on Zimbabwe for intimidating opposition party supporters, judges, journalists and white farmers, and manipulating presidential election; calls on Britain to compensate white farmers who have been forced to give up their farms without compensation; comments draw outrage from supporters of Zimbabwe's opposition party and many political analysts in South Africa (M)

2002-11-17.title: Peruvian Leader's Party Fears Setback in Regional Voting
2002-11-17.abstract: Peru Possible, party of Pres Alejandro Toledo, is expected to suffer stinging setback in nationwide elections intended to decentralize Peru's government with creation of 25 new government bodies; Toledo plays down his troubles, explaining that his government has had difficult task of solidifying democracy while satisfying needs during his 16 months in power; latest poll shows his approval rating at 23 percent, up from 11 percent earlier this year; photo; map (M)

2002-11-17.title: Turkey Turns To Moderate In Islamic Party As New Premier
2002-11-17.abstract: Abdullah Gul, moderate politician from party with Muslim roots, is named prime minister of Turkey, and he expresses confidence that country's democracy and its Western leanings can thrive under Islamic leadership; affirms Tukey's friendship with US and its membership in NATO (M)

2002-11-17.title: As Settlers Pray for the Dead, Israel Weighs Retaliation
2002-11-17.abstract: Israeli settlers hold services for 12 people killed in ambush in Hebron on Nov 15; victims were five border police soldiers, four regular army soldiers and three civilian security guards from nearby Israeli settlement of Kiryat Arba; Islamic Jihad has claimed responsibility for attack, calling it retaliation for Israel's killing of its local leader in West Bank city of Jenin; Israeli leaders are considering their response; photo (M)

2002-11-18.title: Booker Prize Won't Include U.S. Writers
2002-11-18.abstract: Organizers of $75,000 annual Booker Prize, Britain's premier literary award, agree to maintain rules that permit only British, Commonwealth and Irish writers to compete; organizers are contemplating introducing second prize--to be awarded less frequently--for lifetime literary achievement, and that could be open to Americans (M)

2002-11-18.title: Baghdad Journal; When It Comes to Iraq's Boss, What Is Face Value?
2002-11-18.abstract: Salaam Abid, portrait painter, has devoted his career to painting Pres Saddam Hussein; he complains that business has been slow since new business opened on sidewalk opposite his gallery in Baghdad, offering lifesize, backlit photographs of Hussein, reproduced on all-weather fabric and mounted on aluminum frames; photographs are new fashion among those eager to have Hussein ever-present in their offices, courtyards and living rooms; Abid is scornful of photographs; photo (M)

2002-11-18.title: Korean Kidnappings: The South's Wound
2002-11-18.abstract: News from Japan about citizens kidnapped by North Korea many years ago enrages relatives of nearly 500 South Koreans who went missing over last quarter century, and are believed to be held against their will in North Korea; South Korean government, unlike Japan, has never made public issue of kidnappings, eager to defuse tensions with its heavily armed neighbor; it has never demanded their return, even as it provided hundreds of millions of dollars in aid to North in recent years; photo (M)e

2002-11-18.title: Attempted Hijacking Foiled Aboard an Israeli Airliner
2002-11-18.abstract: Knife-wielding man tries to hijack Israeli passenger jet from Tel Aviv minutes before its landing in Istanbul, but he is tackled by air marshals as he tries to force his way into pilots' cabin; nobody on board El Al jet is injured; Turkey's state-run news agency identifies suspect as Tevfik Fukra, 23-year-old Israeli Arab; Turkish police are interrogating him trying to determine motives behind attack; attempted hijacking is unusual for El Al, widely regarded as world's most heavily guarded airline; photo (M)

2002-11-18.title: Venezuelan President's Troops Try to Tighten Noose Around Caracas Mayor's Police Forces
2002-11-18.abstract: Venezuelan troops try to consolidate their hold on 8,000-member police force in Caracas, day after Pres Hugo Chavez's government orders management of department taken away from Mayor Alfredo Pena, staunch opponent of president; tension rises in country and it remains unclear how much of grip Chavez's government has on Police Dept; Pena claims several top-ranking police officials remain loyal to him and to his police chief; photos (M)

2002-11-19.title: Labor Party in Israel Names Former General as Its Leader
2002-11-19.abstract: The Labor Party chose as its standard-bearer Amram Mitzna, who campaigned on immediate peace negotiations.

2002-11-19.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Body Of Man Executed In U.S. Is Back
2002-11-19.abstract: Body of Mir Aimal Kasi, executed in Virginia, is welcomed home by hundreds in Quetta, Pakistan; he was convicted of killing two CIA employees in US in 1993 (S)

2002-11-19.title: Oil From Crippled Ship Fouling Beaches in Spain
2002-11-19.abstract: Clean-up crews begin removing sludge from beaches in northwestern Spain fouled when stricken tanker Prestige leaked over 3,000 tons of fuel oil into Atlantic; spill threatens birds, marine life and livelihood of thousands of fishermen in region famous for seafood; calmer weather has allowed tugs to tow tanker, which began to leak again, to area southwest of coast; ship will remain serious environmental threat until its 77,000 tons of fuel oil can be loaded onto another tanker; Spain has refused ship access to any port and seeks to exploit crisis in talks with Britain on future of Gibraltar, British colony claimed by Spain; Britain denies Gibraltar has any link to accident (M)

2002-11-19.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Trial For G.I. Sergeant
2002-11-19.abstract: United States Sergeant Fernando Nino's court-martial begins in South Korea; stands accused of negligent homicide in death of two local teenage girls; his vehicle struck girls during military exercise (S)

2002-11-19.title: Islamic Allies In Pakistan Fail in Talks For Coalition
2002-11-19.abstract: islamic allies in pakistan fail in talks f

2002-11-20.title: Setbacks Dealt to Iraqi Opposition in Europe
2002-11-20.abstract: Iraqi opposition groups move conference on replacing Saddam Hussein's government from Belgium to London and delay it until mid-Dec amid quarreling and reduced expectations; proposed new date and place emerged only after several groups met with team from State Department, Pentagon and National Security Council, which pressed them to resolve differences and reconsider agenda; opposition leader Sharif Ali bin al-Hussein says up to 400 delegates may be invited to represent wide range of groups; Belgium reportedly blocked conference because opposition's goal of toppling Saddam Hussein is beyond United Nation's goal of disarming him; former Iraqi Gen Nizar al-Khazraji, who defected in 1995, is charged in Denmark with taking part in war crimes for his supposed role in supervising officer who killed Iraqi civilians in 1988 during campaign in which as many as 182,000 Kurds were killed; photo (M)

2002-11-20.title: Venezuelan Marchers Want Police Restored to Civilian Rule
2002-11-20.abstract: Thousands of middle-class Venezuelans opposed to Pres Hugo Chavez and his miliary takeover of Caracas police march peacefully through city demanding force be restored to civilian control; are jeered by Chavez supporters, who are eventually dispersed by National Guard troops to let delegation of 12 mayors and governors deliver statement to National Assembly; Caracas Mayor Alfredo Pena urges Chavez to submit to nonbiding referendum; threatens new national strike; photo (M)

2002-11-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: G.I. Found Not Guilty
2002-11-20.abstract: United States Army Sergeant Fernando Nino is acquitted of negligent homicide; stood trial for deaths of two local teenage girls struck by his vehicle during military excercise (S)

2002-11-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Charges Of Highway Robbery
2002-11-20.abstract: Thirty-seven people are arrested in Italy for embezzlement of public works funds allotted to modernize Salerno-Reggio Calabria highway; police suspect Calabrian mob is involved in crime; map (S)

2002-11-20.title: Oil Tanker Splits Apart Off Spain, Threatening Coast
2002-11-20.abstract: Communities along Spain's northern coast gird for widespread oil contamination as crippled tanker Prestige splits in two and sinks over 100 miles offshore; about 2 million gallons of its nearly 20-million-gallon load spills when tanker founders after leaking trail of fuel oil for six days from widening crack in its unlined hull; environmental groups call for accelerating shift to sturdier tanker designs with double hulls; sinking raises questions about Spain's decision to order disabled ship towed offshore, where its load could now threaten broader region; experts say it could take week for newly spilled oil to reach shore; say 90 percent of load that sank with ship is likely to congeal in deep water, but could rise to warmer surface and liquefy again if compartments shatter when hull hits bottom; table of major oil spills; map; photo (M)

2002-11-21.title: Several Nations Weigh U.S. Request to Join Forces Against Iraq
2002-11-21.abstract: The governments of Britain, Australia, Denmark and several other nations said today that they were strongly considering formal requests from the United States to join a military coalition against Iraq, if weapons inspections fail.    The countries are among more than 50 nations that American diplomats have been contacting this week in foreign capitals and at the NATO summit meeting in Prague to discuss participating either in an invasion or postwar reconstruction effort in Iraq, administration officials said.

2002-11-21.title: American Missionary Is Shot Dead in Lebanon
2002-11-21.abstract: A young American evangelist was shot dead today at a clinic where she worked as a nurse's assistant in Lebanon.

2002-11-21.title: A Call to Arms: Bush's Plea on Iraq
2002-11-21.abstract: Pres Bush's speech in Prague on using military force, if needed, to disarm Iraq was less rousing then his Sept speech to United Nations, apparently because NATO is not prepared politically and militarily for fight; Germany remains greatest political obstacle (M)

2002-11-21.title: World Business Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Shots Fired At Northern Boat
2002-11-21.abstract: South Korean Navy boat fires warning to North Korean patrol which crossed sea border into south; no fire is returned and vessel withdraws to North Korean waters (S)

2002-11-21.title: Global Terrorism Warning
2002-11-21.abstract: State Department, responding to latest Osama bin Laden audiotape, warns Americans around world may be terrorist targets (S)

2002-11-22.title: Indonesians, Tracing A.T.M. Use, Arrest Major Suspect in Bali Bombing
2002-11-22.abstract: Indonesian authorities use ATM records to trace and arrest Imam Samudra, major suspect in Bali nightclub bombing in October; photo (M)

2002-11-22.title: Anger Spreads With Oil Spill Along Imperiled Spanish Coast
2002-11-22.abstract: Fierce winds blow oil slicks from shipwrecked tanker Prestige closer to coast of northwest Spain, where viscous black fuel oil has washed up on beaches south of 150 miles of coast already contaminated; map; fishing and tourism are hard hit and local officials are bitterly critical of central government's efforts (M)

2002-11-22.title: British Firefighters Back on Strike as Pay Talks Collapse
2002-11-22.abstract: Britain's 50,000 firefighters returned to the picket lines on an eight-day strike today after talks broke down.

2002-11-22.title: NATO LEADERS SAY IRAQ MUST DISARM
2002-11-22.abstract: Nineteen NATO leaders, meeting in Prague, unite behind Pres Bush and strongly condemn Iraq for failing to meet UN demands to surrender weapons of mass destruction; Germany's continuing opposition to war will likely prevent alliance from formally entering campaign, although many members appear willing to do so on ad hoc basis; leaders agree to establish rapid reaction force that could move swiftly around globe and keep NATO relevant in fight against terrorism; final session hears Latvian Pres Vaira Vike-Freiberga thank leaders for admitting raft of militarily weak nations craving stability that Poland, Hungary and Czech Republic have enjoyed since their admission in 1999; Latvian leader points to her country's long loss of independence and recalls growing up as refugee until she settled in Canada, where she was noted psychologist; photos; map; political split on Iraq is evident as seven nations formally invited into NATO join with aspiring members Albania, Croatia and Macedonia to issue muscular declarations on Iraq; German-American rift preoccupies meeting; Pres Bush and Chancellor Gerhard Schroder twice shake hands and exchange pleasantries but Min Joschka Fischer reiterates opposition to military action (M)

2002-11-22.title: Art or Ghoulishness? Autopsy Is TV Spectacle in Britain
2002-11-22.abstract: Latest television reality show in Britain is on-air autopsy performed by German doctor, Gunther von Hagens, with 'almost droning commentary'; scene takes place beneath large copy of Rembrandt's Anatomy Lesson of Dr Nicolaes Tulp; photos; von Hagens could face prosecution for violating laws dating to 1832 that banned public autopsies to stop body snatching; his current exhibit, Body Worlds, consisting of preserved cross sections of human cadavers, has drawn million visitors to London gallery (M)

2002-11-23.title: AtHomeAbroad: When life in a different culture becomes a time for self-invention
2002-11-23.abstract: BERLIN—  Holly-Jane Rahlens, a novelist, director and performance artist, has lived in Berlin for 30 years. Her just-published young adult novel, "Prince William, Maximilian Minsky and Me," has received outstanding reviews in Germany, and the nice Jewish girl from Queens has grown into the able chronicler of Jewish life in Berlin.

2002-11-23.title: PUTIN QUESTIONS U.S. TERROR ALLIES
2002-11-23.abstract: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia welcomed President Bush to his hometown today, then raised pointed questions about the reliability of two important American allies in the campaign against terrorism, Pakistan and Saudi Arabia.    In remarks made at Mr. Bush's side in the majestic setting of Catherine Palace outside St. Petersburg, Mr. Putin also suggested that the disappearance of Osama bin Laden, the leader of Al Qaeda, was a major piece of unfinished business as the Bush administration focused its attention on a possible war in Iraq.

2002-11-23.title: New Belarus Law Codifies Rising Religious Repression
2002-11-23.abstract: In the last four months, Tatyana and Sergei Akadanovy have been arrested twice, sent to jail for 10 days and fined more than $1,000, an unimaginable sum in impoverished Belarus.    An apartment they help rent has been broken into and vandalized. Mrs. Akadanova has been severely beaten on the steps of their apartment, a fate that separately befell six friends, and the police have issued warnings that the Akadanovys and their friends are all criminals who should be avoided.

2002-11-23.title: World Briefing | Americas: Venezuela: Opposition Sets Strike Date
2002-11-23.abstract: The opposition coalition seeking a referendum on President Hugo Chávez announced that it would mount a nationwide general strike on Dec. 2, or earlier if conditions warrant. It would be the fourth general strike since December in the conflict between Mr. Chavéz and his opponents. The announcement was the latest setback to the talks between the government and the opposition, which are mediated by the Organization of American States.   David Gonzalez (NYT)

2002-11-23.title: Mounties Dig Up Body Parts in Serial Killing Case
2002-11-23.abstract: Before his arrest, Robert William Pickton kept several menacing signs outside his pig farm, including one that read: ''No Visitors, Agents, Peddlers or Salespeople -- Admittance by Appointment Only!! (No Exceptions).'' Another said, ''This property is protected by: Pit Bull With AIDS.''    The police and prosecutors say he had reason to be cautious. The remains of 18 women have been found on his 15-acre property in a nearby suburb, and investigators say many more may turn up before they are through.

2002-11-24.title: South American Trading Bloc Frees Movement of Its People
2002-11-24.abstract: Six member nations of South American common market called Mercosur approve plan that will allow their 250 million people to live and work in any other member country and be granted same rights as citizens of those nations; Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay and Uruguay are founders and full members; Bolivia and Chile joined later and are associate members; six countries have combined gross product of more than $1 trillion; Mercosur is world's third-largest trade group (M)

2002-11-24.title: Aquarium in Spain Faces Disaster With Oil Spilled From Ship at Its Walls
2002-11-24.abstract: Oil from stricken tanker Prestige is threatening aquarium at La Coruna, Spain, which fills its tanks with seawater; fuel oil shed by elderly tanker Prestige along coast of Galicia for six days threatens city of La Coruna as well; aquarium's tanks have not yet been polluted, but omens are not good; two resident seals are stained with oil; aquarium could survive for three days without pumping in any seawater; many of 35,000 fish might survive transfer elsewhere, but not 62 species of invertebrates; photo; map (M)

2002-11-24.title: Israel Admits One of Its Soldiers Killed U.N. Official in Jenin
2002-11-24.abstract: Israeli military admits that it was shot fired by one of its soldiers that killed senior United Nations official Iain John Hook at agency's compound in Jenin refugee camp in West Bank; Israeli statement claims that Palestinian gunmen had been firing at soldiers from inside small UN compound; this is potentially serious accusation given implication that UN officials sheltered gunmen in some way; charge is likely to increase friction between Israeli government and UN (M)

2002-11-24.title: Saudis Say Cash to Friend Of Hijackers Was Charity
2002-11-24.abstract: Government of Saudi Arabia says wife of Saudi ambassador to Washington gave tens of thousands of dollars in what she believed were charitable gifts to family of Saudi man in San Diego who befriended and assisted two of Sept 11 hijackers; Saudi officials and their American friends say payments from Princess Haifa al-Faisal, wife of Amb Bandar bin Sultan, began four years ago, and they were typical of charitable gifts she provided to Saudis in distress, many of them strangers who asked for help by mail; suggestion that money from Saudi royal family may have been used, if only indirectly, to help hijackers and their friends has created alarm in Riyadh and in Washington (M)

2002-11-24.title: Austria Expects Close Election As Far Right Yields to Center
2002-11-24.abstract: National elections Nov 24 figure to be among closest in Austria's history, as nation's traditional center-left and center-right parties battle for largest plurality and right to name next chancellor; most intriguing political story concerns certain loser: country's self-destructing right-wing Freedom Party and Jorg Haider, its once magnetic but now increasingly marginalized leader; Haider became international pariah during 1990's for praising Hitler's labor policies; political analysts say voters have grown tired of his antics; photo (M)

2002-11-25.title: German Comedy Lances the Nazis, but 'The Producers' Is Safe
2002-11-25.abstract: German television broadcasts first German-made film that tries to play country's darkest period for laughs; 90-minute farce 'Goebbels and Geduldig' is about Nazi propaganda minister and Jewish concentration camp inmate who looks identical to him; until not, poking fun at Nazis has been taboo in Germany; critics pan film, saying it is not funny (M)

2002-11-25.title: Philip Denies Calling Diana Vulgar Names
2002-11-25.abstract: Queen Elizabeth's husband Prince Philip makes public denial of news reports that he sent Princess Diana insulting letters calling her 'harlot' and 'trollop'; report first appeared in one British newspaper and was picked up by every other newspaper in Britain's fiercely competitive press (S)

2002-11-25.title: Senators Assail Saudis
2002-11-25.abstract: Top Republicans and Democrats criticized the role of the Saudis in the campaign against terrorism, and suggested that the administration was too timid in confronting them.   Article, Page A13.

2002-11-25.title: Whistler Journal; Repair of British Columbia Road Is Olympic Event
2002-11-25.abstract: Provincial government of British Columbia embarks on one of most expensive engineeering projects in Canada's history, to widen and improve Route 99, treacherous road called Highway of Death in local news media; Vancouver fears that highway's deadly reputation could threaten its bid to host 2010 Winter Olympic games; alpine resort of Whistler, off Route 99, would host many events; photo; map (M)

2002-11-25.title: Israeli Soldiers Fire on Crowd, Killing Boy
2002-11-25.abstract: It was one of several clashes today in Nablus, in which a reported seven people were injured by fire from Israeli tanks and guns.

2002-11-26.title: U.S., Citing Maoist Threats, Warns Against Visits to Nepal
2002-11-26.abstract: US State Dept urges Americans to defer nonessential travel to Nepal because of written threat from Maoist rebels to attack American diplomats (S)

2002-11-26.title: Iran Forbids Demonstrations Defending a Reformist Scholar
2002-11-26.abstract: Iran's National Security Council reportedly bars students from further demonstrations backing Hashem Aghajari, pro-reform scholar sentenced to death, after supreme religious leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei orders review of case; students vow to press their demands through other peaceful means (M)

2002-11-26.title: World Briefing | Asia: Hong Kong: Tanker Still Burning
2002-11-26.abstract: Tanker carrying liquid petroleum gas continues to burn twenty-four miles from Hong Kong; winds continue to keep fire from flamable cargo (S)

2002-11-26.title: U.S. Fails to Curb Its Saudi Oil Habit, Experts Say
2002-11-26.abstract: America remains as dependent as ever on Saudi Arabia for oil, nearly 12 years after Persian Gulf war when reliance on Saudi supplies prompted calls for US to diversify sources; Saudi Arabia supplies approximately one-sixth of US imports, but has considerable political strength as only producer with spare capacity to replace millions of barrels daily, which could be drained from market by US military action against Iraq; produces approximately eight million barrels per day; officials say it could raise output to 10 million barrels fairly quickly and to 10.5 million within three months; US Energy Department official Guy Caruso says if war halts Iraq's exports of between 1.5 million to 2 million barrels daily, situation would be manageable; says it would be harder to replace steeper decline in exports, which would occur if exports from other Persian Gulf suppliers are reduced by terrorist attacks or by prohibitive insurance premiums on oil tankers; graphs; chart (M)

2002-11-26.title: World Briefing | Asia: Kashmir: Caution And Blame After Attack
2002-11-26.abstract: Attack on temple in Jammu, India, prompts curfew in Kashmir's winter capital; Indian government blames Pakistan-based militants for assault (S)

2002-11-27.title: French Link 6 to 'Shoe Bomb' Attempt; 12 Other Militants Seized in Paris Raids
2002-11-27.abstract: French antiterrorist police arrest six men thought to belong to Islamic militant network that supported Richard C Reid, Briton who admitted trying to blow up American Airlines trans-Atlantic flight last Dec with explosives hidden in his shoes; predawn raid is latest in four waves of arrests in four days, as police remain on high alert to thwart possible attacks by radical Islamic groups; worshippers complain about raid on Islamic center, where Imam Kamel Din was among those detained; photo (M)

2002-11-27.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-11-27.abstract: AUSTRIA: Haider Flip-Flops On Resignation; NORTHERN IRELAND: Arrests On Both Sides; DENMARK: Chechen'S Detention Extended; RUSSIA: Return Of A `Sacred' Star; BELARUS: At Least One Friend, Maybe;.

2002-11-27.title: Women With H.I.V. Reach Half of Global Cases
2002-11-27.abstract: United Nations AIDS program head Dr Peter Piot says women for first time make up about half of adults infected with HIV worldwide, chiefly as result of sexual intercourse with infected men; 19.2 million of 38.6 million adults infected with AIDS virus are women; figures largely reflect epidemic in sub-Saharan Africa, where nearly 1 in 11 adults are infected and women account for 58 percent of infections; similar parity has been reached in Caribbean, North Africa and Mideast; graph of percentage of infected women by region (M)

2002-11-27.title: West Bank Explosion Kills 2 'Most Wanted'
2002-11-27.abstract: House in Jenin refugee camp explodes, killinging suspected high-level Palestinian militants Ala Sabaar and Aimad Nasharta; Palestinian witnesses blame missile fired from Israeli military aircraft; says Israeli tanks and armored vehicles entered West Bank camp just before blast; military official says Army did not fire on house; Israeli military activity in West Bank and Gaza has picked up since suicide attack in Jerusalem; Israeli military releases recording of phone message United Nations aide Iain John Hook reportedly left with Israeli civil officals minutes before he was shot in Jenin by Israeli soldiers; apparently seeks to bolster its claims that Palestinian gunmen had been firing on Israeli troops from inside UN compound; photo (M)

2002-11-27.title: After Oil Spill, Spain and France Impose Strict Tanker Inspections
2002-11-27.abstract: Spanish Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar and French Pres Jacques Chirac, spurred by oil spill from tanker Prestige, set rigourous inspection standards for older single-hulled tankers carrying oil or tar through waters their nations control; empower maritime authorities to inspect ships up to 200 miles out to sea and to expel those not considered seaworthy from 200-mile zone (M)

2002-11-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: Sri Lanka: Rebel Leader Demands Autonomy
2002-11-28.abstract: Tamil leader Velupillai Prabhakaran declares rebels will make attempt at secession from Sri Lanka if group is not given autonomy, speech to his guerrilla fighters (S)

2002-11-28.title: 'Clear as Glass' Against War With Iraq, Germans Still Agree to Aid U.S. and Israel
2002-11-28.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroder says that he will grant United States unrestricted freedom to fly in German airspace, as well as to shuttle troops in and out of American military bases there; reiterates, however, that Germany will not take part in war against Iraq; also says Germany would supply Israel with German-made armored vehicles and Patriot antimissile system to protect it from Iraqi chemical, biological or nuclear attack (M)

2002-11-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Tycoon Charged With Corruption
2002-11-28.abstract: Entrepreneur and Chinese national Yang Bin is arrested by Chinese authorities and charged with bribery and fraud; also faces regional charges in Shenyang for illegally occupying farmland; charges have stopped him from assuming duties as head of new North Korean zone near China that was devised to attract foreign investors; photo (S)

2002-11-28.title: In Kenya, 3 Suicide Bombers Attack Hotel Owned by Israelis; Missiles Fired at Passenger Jet
2002-11-28.abstract: Three suicide car bombers killed themselves and 12 others at an Israeli-owned hotel in Mombasa, Kenya. Missiles fired at an Israeli airliner missed their target.

2002-11-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Belgium: Fishing Ban Considered
2002-11-28.abstract: Proposed bans on cod and haddock catches are to be debated by European Union fisheries ministers; European Fisheries Commissioner Franz Fischler supports cuts to preserve industry while protecting stocks; photo (S)

2002-11-29.title: Fiery Zealotry Leaves Nigeria in Ashes Again
2002-11-29.abstract: Nigerian Pres Olusegun Obasanjo visits Kaduna in wake of clashes between Muslims and Christians; visits riot victims; religious strife, which has erupted with some regularity in region, is not likely to end with decision to move Miss World beauty contest from Abuja to London; map; photos (M)

2002-11-29.title: Fight Against Terror: Two Conflicts or One?
2002-11-29.abstract: Question of whether Israelis attacked in Kenya and while voting in Israel, surely victims of at least two different organizations, are victims of same worldwide terror drive goes to heart of Bush administration's widening fight against terrorism and Israel's focused conflict with Palestinians; United States and Israel have drawn ever closer since Sept 11 attacks, but differ over whether treating two conflicts as one and same would help or hinder in resolving either fight; Israelis see Kenya attacks, in which Israelis have fallen victims to terrorism with global reach, showing fallacy of Bush criticism of Israeli efforts against Palestinian terrorism that are similar to US antiterrorist efforts (M)

2002-11-29.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Leftists Accused Of Terrorism Arrested
2002-11-29.abstract: Spanish police hold seven people suspected to be members of far-left group Grapo, which has been labeled terrorist organization by US and European Union (S)

2002-11-29.title: Venezuela Court Blocks Order for Nonbinding Vote on President
2002-11-29.abstract: Venezuela's Supreme Court apparently blocks move by Electoral Council to hold nonbiding referendum on whether Pres Hugo Chavez should resign; finds council needed at least 4-1 majority to act, rather than simple majority it mustered; Chavez's government rejects council move (M)2

2002-11-29.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Four Charged In Gun Plot
2002-11-29.abstract: Two men and two women in Northern Ireland are charged with 1999 funding of IRA guns and ammunition smuggled from US (S)

2002-11-30.title: I.M.F. Skewers Corruption in Angola
2002-11-30.abstract: International Monetary Fund, in unusually blunt confidential report, documents disappearance of almost $1 billion from Angola's treasury in 2001; cites widespread corruption; Angoloan officials reportedly are furious about report, which was completed in Mar and widely circulated among donor nations and multilateral organizations; need IMF support to raise funds from donor nations they say are needed to rebuild war-ravaged infrastructure (M)

2002-11-30.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: Financial Scandal Uncovered
2002-11-30.abstract: Japan's Foreign Minister Yoriko Kawaguchi reports that ministry has disciplined 39 officials who doctored expense reports defrauding government of almost $4 million dollars; Vice Minister Yukio Takeuchi and Deputy Vice Minister Shinichi Kitajima are censured; last year 328 officials were similarly exposed and punished (S)

2002-11-30.title: Wide Reach Of Terrorism
2002-11-30.abstract: Disrupting terrorist attacks is becoming even more daunting as terrorist increasingly attack 'soft' targets like Israeli-owned Mombasa, Kenya, resort; at issue is deciding where to focus security and intelligence resources if enemy is able to strike almost anywhere; intelligence officials around world say they have become much more aggressive in collecting and distributing intelligence tips, even about unlikely threats; Australian officials say they got reports two weeks ago about threat in Mombasa, leading them to urge citizens not to go there; say all such data is automatically passed to United States; American officials say they were unaware of any credible and specific data that could have predicted attacks on hotel and on Israeli plane (M)

2002-11-30.title: Does Schröder Have Political Capital to Reform Economy?
2002-11-30.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, who has long promised economic reforms, faces questions about whether he has courage and political capital to tackle woes with his popularity falling and economy tipping toward second recession in year; pressure to address problems is coming from within Germany and from nation's European Union partners; labor unions and other vested interests that form base of Schoder's support have stymied reform and upcoming elections in two states make it hard for Schroder or opposition to propose unpopular measures; graph; photo (M)

2002-11-30.title: Shuffling 'Heirs' to Cambodia Throne
2002-11-30.abstract: Question of succession to Cambodia's throne, once taboo, has recently become subject of debate and speculation, prodded by King Norodom Sihanouk himself, who is 80 and ailing; hundreds of descendants of any of three past kings are eligible under 1993 Constitution; supporters of Prime Min Hun Sen control Throne Council, which will choose next king; Hun Sen has blocked bill clarifying succession process, but cannot simply ignore wishes of popular king; Prince Norodom Sihamoni, one of king's many sons, has emerged as strong possibility, even though he spends much of his time in Paris and has taken little interest in royal affairs; photo (M)
